content:
<USER_REQUEST>
Prompt:

Build a futuristic web application called AirDraw that allows users to draw in mid-air using hand gestures captured from their webcam.

Core Requirements
Use MediaPipe Hands for real-time hand tracking.
Use Three.js for rendering a 3D scene.
Request webcam access when the application starts.
Track the user's hand landmarks at 60 FPS if possible.
Create a floating 3D drawing in space by following the tip of the index finger.
Drawing Controls
Index finger extended only → Draw mode.
Open palm → Stop drawing.
Closed fist → Eraser mode.
Thumb and index finger pinch → Select nearest drawing.
Pinch and drag → Move selected drawing.
Two-hand pinch gesture → Scale selected drawing.
Pinch + wrist rotation → Rotate selected drawing.
Three fingers extended → Cycle through brush colors.
Peace sign → Create a new drawing layer.
Drawing System
Drawings must exist as true 3D objects, not 2D canvas strokes.
Store every stroke as a collection of 3D points.
Render smooth curves between points.
Support multiple independent strokes.
Allow undo and redo.
Allow deleting individual strokes.
Allow clearing the entire scene.
Visual Design

Create a modern holographic interface inspired by sci-fi movies.

Features:

Dark futuristic background.
Neon blue and purple glow effects.
Glassmorphism control panels.
Animated particle effects around the cursor.
Smooth transitions and animations.
Responsive design for desktop and mobile.
Extra Features
Brush size adjustment using hand gestures.
Color palette controlled by gestures.
Glow brush mode.
Particle brush mode.
Save artwork as JSON.
Export artwork as GLTF.
Import previously saved drawings.
Screenshot capture.
Fullscreen mode.
Performance
Optimize for smooth real-time interaction.
Use requestAnimationFrame.
Avoid memory leaks.
Handle lost hand tracking gracefully.
Display tracking confidence indicators.
Project Structure

Organize code into modules:

src/
├── handTracking.js
├── gestureRecognition.js
├── drawingEngine.js
├── objectManipulation.js
├── sceneManager.js
├── ui.js
├── exportManager.js
└── main.js
Deliverables
Complete working source code.
Modern folder structure.
Detailed comments.
Clean reusable components.
Professional production-ready implementation.
No placeholder functionality.
All gestures fully implemented.

The final result should feel like a real-world "Minority Report" style holographic air-drawing system where users can draw, move, rotate, resize, and erase floating 3D artwork entirely through hand gestures.
</USER_REQUEST>

Since **AirDraw** is a purely client-side static web application (HTML, CSS, and JS), it is extremely easy to host. Here are the best free platforms and how to deploy to them:

### 1. Vercel (Highly Recommended — Easiest & Fastest)
Vercel is designed specifically for frontend hosting and integrates seamlessly with Vite projects.
* **Via GitHub (Automatic)**:
  1. Push your code to a GitHub repository.
  2. Sign in to [Vercel](https://vercel.com/) and click **Add New** > **Project**.
  3. Import your repository. Vercel will automatically detect the Vite template and configure the build command (`npm run build`) and output directory (`dist`). Click **Deploy**.
* **Via CLI**:
  Run this command in the `C:\Users\mansi\.gemini\antigravity\scratch\airdraw` directory:
  ```powershell
  npm install -g vercel
  vercel
  ```

### 2. GitHub Pages (Free & Directly in your Repository)
If you already use GitHub, you can publish the app directly from your repository.
1. Push your project to GitHub.
2. Go to your repository settings > **Pages**.
3. Under **Build and deployment**, select **GitHub Actions** as the source.
4. Choose the **Static HTML** template or configure a workflow that runs `npm run build` and deploys the generated `dist/` directory.

### 3. Netlify (Drag and Drop)
You can deploy without pushing to Git:
1. Run `npm run build` in your project folder to generate the compiled files.
2. Log in to [Netlify](https://www.netlify.com/).
3. Drag and drop the generated `dist/` folder directly into the Netlify deployment box.

The **404 File Not Found** error is occurring due to two common reasons when hosting a Vite project on GitHub Pages:
1. **Asset Path Mismatch**: By default, Vite builds assuming it is hosted at the root domain (`/`). Since your project is hosted in a subfolder (`/antigravity/`), the browser looks at `mansimratpannu.github.io/assets/...` instead of `mansimratpannu.github.io/antigravity/assets/...` and fails.
2. **Uploading Source Instead of Build**: GitHub Pages does not automatically build Vite apps. You must publish the compiled output folder (`dist/`), not the raw source repository.

---

### 🛠️ What I Did to Fix This:
1. **Created Vite Config**: I created [vite.config.js](file:///C:/Users/mansi/.gemini/antigravity/scratch/airdraw/vite.config.js) with `base: '/antigravity/'` to make sure all built assets compile with correct relative path subfolders.
2. **Added Automated Deploy Scripts**: I installed the standard `gh-pages` utility and updated your [package.json](file:///C:/Users/mansi/.gemini/antigravity/scratch/airdraw/package.json) to include deployment scripts:
   * `"predeploy": "npm run build"` (automatically compiles prior to uploading).
   * `"deploy": "gh-pages -d dist"` (automatically pushes the compiled build folder to your repository's `gh-pages` branch).

---

### 🚀 How to Deploy Now:
1. Open your terminal, navigate to the project folder:
   ```powershell
   cd C:\Users\mansi\.gemini\antigravity\scratch\airdraw
   ```
2. Run the deployment script (make sure your local repository is initialized and connected to your GitHub remote first):
   ```powershell
   npm run deploy
   ```
3. Go to your GitHub repository (**`antigravity`**) Settings > **Pages**.
4. Under **Build and deployment**, set the source branch to **`gh-pages`** (and folder to `/ (root)`). Save.

Once GitHub finishes deploying (takes ~1 minute), reload your website page at **[mansimratpannu.github.io/antigravity/](https://mansimratpannu.github.io/antigravity/)**!

"# Implementation Plan - Gesture Detection & Drawing Fixes

This plan aims to resolve the drawing detection issues ("cant draw", "index point not working", and "new layer peace sign not working") by switching the finger extension heuristic from a wrist-relative distance calculation to a knuckle-relative (MCP joint) 3D Euclidean distance calculation.

## User Review Required

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Knuckle-Relative Extension Heuristic**:
> Instead of using the wrist (landmark 0) as the reference point for finger extension, we will use each finger's corresponding knuckle (MCP joint, landmark `pipIdx - 1`). 
> - **Extended Ratio (straight finger)**: The tip-to-knuckle distance is about `2.0x` to `2.5x` of the PIP-to-knuckle distance.
> - **Curled Ratio (folded finger)**: The tip curls back to the knuckle, dropping the ratio below `1.0x`.
> - This offers a massive, stable separation (2.0 vs < 1.0) compared to the old wrist-based ratio (1.23 vs 0.85), making it highly resilient to depth noise.

---

## Proposed Changes

### 1. Gesture Recognition Heuristics

#### [MODIFY] [gestureRecognition.js](file:///C:/Users/mansi/.gemini/antigravity/scratch/airdraw/src/gestureRecognition.js)
- Update `isFingerExtended(landmarks, tipIdx, pipIdx)` to:
  - Derive `mcpIdx = pipIdx - 1`.
  - Calculate `mcpToPip` and `mcpToTip` using 3D Euclidean distance.
  - Return `true` if `mcpToTip > mcpToPip * 1.35`.
- Remove the unused `wristIdx` parameter from `isFingerExtended`.

### 2. Coordinate Sanitization & Safety Guards

#### [MODIFY] [drawingEngine.js](file:///C:/Users/mansi/.gemini/antigravity/scratch/airdraw/src/drawingEngine.js)
- Add safety guards in `addPointToStroke(point)` to filter out any coordinates containing `NaN` values before performing operations or appending to `activeStrokePoints`.

#### [MODIFY] [main.js](file:///C:/Users/mansi/.gemini/antigravity/scratch/airdraw/src/main.js)
- Add debug logging to `executeGestureState` to write active modes to the HUD System Console when they change,
